summary:
Mycenaean palace culture emerged in mainland Greece between approximately 1600 and 1100 BC, building on earlier Helladic traditions to create the first literate, state-organized civilization on European soil. The major palace centers at Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos, Thebes, and Athens were massive stone-built complexes organized around a central throne room called the megaron, surrounded by storerooms, workshops, and administrative offices. These palaces functioned simultaneously as royal residences, economic redistribution centers, religious sanctuaries, and military headquarters.

The [[event:1823|Linear B script]], adapted from Minoan Linear A, was used by palace scribes to record inventories, tax assessments, personnel rosters, and religious offerings on clay tablets. The tablets from Pylos, destroyed by fire around 1180 BC and thereby accidentally preserved, provide an unusually detailed picture of a Mycenaean palace economy. The palace controlled the production and distribution of textiles, bronze weapons, oil, grain, and livestock across a substantial territory, with a hierarchy of administrators, collectors, and local officials managing the flow of goods.

Mycenaean art and architecture were distinctive and impressive: the Lion Gate at Mycenae, the Treasury of Atreus beehive tomb, and the frescoes depicting processions, hunting scenes, and warfare found at multiple sites all testify to a sophisticated aristocratic culture. The [[event:1895|shaft graves at Mycenae]] contained extraordinary concentrations of gold objects, including masks, cups, and weapons, indicating the wealth accumulated by early Mycenaean warrior elites. Palace culture collapsed suddenly around 1180 to 1100 BC as part of the broader [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]], leaving behind ruins that later Greeks attributed to a heroic age of myth.
